slides.title: The capstone scenario
body: Arlington Property Management (APM) manages condo associations across New England. They've outgrown spreadsheets and want a real Salesforce implementation.
Your job as the consultant: design the data model, decide what's automated in Apex vs. config, plan integrations, plan tests.

slides.title: What APM tracks
body: Associations (each condo building's HOA), Buildings, Units, Owners, Residents, Board Members, Property Managers, Vendors (landscapers, plumbers, etc.), and Work Orders.
Plus the financial pieces: monthly dues, management fees, periodic fee increases.

slides.title: Step 1 — Data model decisions
body: Account is the obvious record type for both Associations AND Vendors — same shape (an organization), different role. Salesforce supports Record Types for this.
Real example: Slack uses a single 'Workspace' object for both customer companies and internal teams — same blueprint, different metadata.

vocabulary.def: Salesforce feature that lets one SObject (like Account) represent multiple business meanings with different page layouts and picklist values.

slides.title: Data model — the relationships
body: Account (Association) → Buildings → Units → Owners (Contact). Account (Vendor) ↔ Association via a Junction object so many vendors can serve many associations.
Real example: this many-to-many junction pattern shows up in EVERY Salesforce build — Opportunities↔Products, Cases↔Contacts, etc.

vocabulary.def: A custom object whose only job is to link two other objects in a many-to-many relationship.

slides.title: The Service__c and Vendor_Service__c objects
body: APM's data model has two custom objects we'll write code against: Service__c (a service offering like 'plumbing' or 'landscaping') and Vendor_Service__c (a junction record linking each vendor Account to one Service).
Real example: this many-to-many junction pattern is exactly how Salesforce ships OpportunityLineItem (Opportunity ↔ Product) — every consulting build will have 1–3 junctions like this.

vocabulary.def: Custom object representing a service offering (plumbing, landscaping, etc.). The __c suffix marks any custom object in Apex.
Junction object linking a vendor Account (Vendor__c lookup) to a Service__c.

slides.title: VendorServiceHelper: getAllServices
slides.intro: Build the heart of Arlington's vendor management — a helper class that returns every available Service. Multi-line SOQL with ORDER BY for predictable Name sorting. The skeleton you'll grow over the next several methods.
lineExplanations: Declare the helper class. 'Helper' is a Salesforce convention — central place for reusable logic that triggers, Flows, and other classes call.
Static method returning a List of Service__c records. Static = call as VendorServiceHelper.getAllServices() with no instance.
Variable receives the SOQL result. Convention: prefix with 'ret' so readers know this is the return value being built.
SELECT clause — five fields including audit columns (LastModifiedDate, CreatedById, OwnerId) callers often need.
FROM the Service__c custom object. The __c suffix marks every custom object in Apex.
ORDER BY Name keeps results predictable for any UI that lists them.
Return the list to the caller.

slides.title: VendorServiceHelper: getServiceById and getServiceByName
slides.intro: Add two single-record fetchers — one keyed by Id, one keyed by Name. Same query shape, different filter. Once you've typed the first, the second feels mechanical, and that repetition is the point: real Salesforce orgs have dozens of these.
lineExplanations: Method takes a Salesforce Id, returns a single Service__c.
Inline SOQL — bind the parameter with :serviceId, LIMIT 1 because we expect one match.
Blank line — Ari's style adds breathing room between query and return.
Same shape, but typed for a String name.
Same SOQL, different WHERE field. NEVER concatenate — bind with :serviceName.

slides.title: VendorServiceHelper: createVendorServicesForSingleVendor
slides.intro: When a vendor signs on for several services at once, build the junction records in bulk: one Vendor_Service__c per service. Bulkified pattern — collect in a List during the loop, ONE DML at the end.
lineExplanations: void method — does work via DML, returns nothing. Two parameters: the single vendor's Account Id, plus a Set of Service Ids.
Empty list to fill — one Vendor_Service__c junction record per service.
Walk every service id. Set<Id> auto-dedupes if the same service was passed twice.
Set the lookup to the vendor (Vendor__c is the lookup field on Vendor_Service__c).
Set the lookup to the service.
Append to the list — no DML yet.
ONE bulk DML statement creates all the junctions at once. This is bulkification done right.

slides.title: VendorServiceHelper: createVendorServicesForSingleService
slides.intro: Mirror image of the previous method — when ONE new service goes live and you need to enable several existing vendors for it. Notice the parameters flipped: now Set<Id> holds vendors, single Id is the service. Same body shape.
lineExplanations: Same shape as the previous method, parameters swapped.
Iterate over the vendor account ids.
Construct a junction record per vendor.
Set the service lookup — same single value every iteration.
ONE bulk insert — same bulkified shape.

slides.title: VendorServiceHelper: generateVendorServiceName
slides.intro: A tiny utility for building consistent display names. Even a three-line method earns its keep when the format is called from five places — change the dash to a slash here and every UI updates at once.
lineExplanations: Static method takes two Strings, returns one String. Public so any class (or trigger or @InvocableMethod) can call it.
Concatenate with ' - ' separator and return. The string between the names is the format — change here, change everywhere.

slides.title: Step 2 — Automation: Apex vs. Flow
body: The brief says ALL automation must be Apex (no Flows). In real consulting, you'd push back: simple click-to-config logic belongs in Flow; Apex is for complex, bulk, or async work.
Real example: 'when a Unit's Owner field changes, recalculate the Association's owner-count' is a one-line Flow rollup. Putting it in Apex is overkill.

slides.title: @InvocableMethod — Apex callable from Flow
body: @InvocableMethod marks a static Apex method as callable from Flow, Process Builder, and Agentforce — the bridge between click-to-config automation and code.

Real example: every Salesforce admin has built a Flow that needed 'one little thing only Apex can do.' @InvocableMethod is the door; Flow walks through it. Pair with @InvocableVariable on inner-class fields when Flow needs to pass complex parameters.

vocabulary.def: Annotation marking a static Apex method as callable from Flow.
Annotation on a public field of an inner class so Flow can pass it into an @InvocableMethod.

slides.title: InvocableServiceFetcher
slides.intro: Wrap getAllServices() so a Flow can call it. The double-list signature (List<List<Service__c>>) is required — Salesforce passes a one-element outer list because @InvocableMethods always run in bulk, even when Flow only invokes them once.
lineExplanations: Declare a class dedicated to Flow-facing wrappers. Convention: 'Invocable' prefix tells future readers this is the Flow surface.
@InvocableMethod above the method — the annotation that exposes it to Flow.
Method must be public static. Returns List<List<...>> — Salesforce's bulk-mode contract.
Delegate to the helper — Flow doesn't care that there's a separate class; the call chain is invisible from Flow's side.
Wrap the inner list inside the outer list.

slides.title: InvocableVendorServiceBuilder
slides.intro: Flow needs to pass TWO inputs (an account id and a list of service ids) into one Apex call. Use an inner class with @InvocableVariable fields as the parameter shape — the standard pattern for any Flow→Apex call carrying more than a single primitive.
lineExplanations: Takes a List of inner-class parameter objects. Even when Flow invokes once, the param is a one-element list.
Pull the first (and usually only) params object out.
Convert the List<Id> Flow gave us into a Set<Id> — the helper expects a Set so duplicates are dedupe'd.
addAll copies all elements from the list into the set in one call.
Delegate to the existing helper — same bulkified path.
Inner class — public so Flow can introspect and bind its fields. This is the parameter SHAPE.
@InvocableVariable on a public field — Flow sees this as an input field on the action.

slides.title: Trigger plan
body: One trigger per object: AccountTrigger, UnitTrigger, ResidentTrigger, WorkOrderTrigger. Each delegates to a handler class.
Handler classes hold the actual logic — testable as plain Apex, no trigger context needed.

slides.title: Trigger handler skeleton
slides.intro: Type this. The exact pattern you'd commit on day one of any new SF org.
lineExplanations: Public static method matching the trigger event.
Look up the old version from the map.
Compare a specific field for change.
Apply your logic — typically queueing an async job or building a List for bulk DML.

slides.title: Step 3 — Async patterns
body: Periodic fee increases across thousands of units → Batchable (chunked processing). 'Send invoice email after dues are billed' → Queueable (async after the trigger). 'Apply 3% rate hike on Jan 1' → Schedulable wrapping the Batchable.
Real example: every multi-tenant property management SaaS (AppFolio, Buildium) does exactly this.

slides.title: Step 4 — Integration plan
body: External vendor invoicing system (Stripe-like): outbound callout from a Queueable when a Work Order completes, posting the invoice to the partner.
All credentials in Named Credentials. No secrets in Apex source. Failures logged to a custom 'Integration Log' object for retry.

slides.title: Step 5 — Caching
body: List of fee schedules per association — read on every dues calculation, changes monthly. Perfect for Custom Metadata (free reads).
User's home Association — needed throughout a session, doesn't change. Static-variable cache for one transaction OR Session Cache for the whole user session.

slides.title: Step 6 — Testing strategy
body: @TestSetup creates 1 Association with 1 Building, 5 Units, 3 Owners. Each test method gets a fresh copy.
Cover happy paths (a unit changes ownership) AND edge cases (owner is null, multiple units in same transaction, hitting governor boundaries with 200 units).

slides.title: Step 7 — Bulkification audit
body: For every trigger handler, ask: what happens when 200 records hit at once? Any SOQL or DML inside a loop? Any callouts inside the trigger?
Real example: this audit catches ~80% of bugs that would otherwise surface on the first big import or batch operation.

slides.front: APM wants a 3% fee increase applied to ~50,000 unit records on Jan 1 every year. What async pattern do you propose?
slides.back: Schedulable triggering a Batchable. The Batchable processes 200 units per chunk; the Schedulable runs once a year via cron.
slides.detail: Synchronous would blow heap and CPU. Queueable doesn't chunk. Batchable's chunking + Schedulable's cron is the textbook fit.

slides.question: APM's brief says 'all automation must be in Apex.' As the consultant, what's your move?
options: Implement everything in Apex without question.
Push back: explain Flow is appropriate for simple click-to-config rules; reserve Apex for complex/bulk/async.
Mix Apex and Flow without telling the client.
slides.explanation: Real consulting work. Push back with rationale. The consultant's value is judgment, not just typing — over-engineering simple cases in Apex creates maintenance debt the client will regret.

slides.title: What you've built
body: A complete mental architecture: data model with record types and junctions, trigger handler pattern, async strategy (queueable/batch/schedule), Named Credentials for integrations, Platform Cache + Custom Metadata, full test coverage.
These are the same building blocks every Salesforce consulting project ever uses.

slides.title: What's next
body: You've finished the core course (matching Ari's original curriculum). The trainer continues with extra topics: Test Classes, Governor Limits, APIs, and Caching — each one a deeper dive into a skill you'll use every day in real consulting work.
Pair the trainer with hands-on work in a real Salesforce sandbox. Type-memorized patterns become muscle when you apply them to actual problems.
